slides.title: Constants 🛡️
slides.content:
When you do not want others (or yourself) to override existing variable values, use the `const` keyword.

This declares the variable as "constant", meaning unchangeable and read-only.

slides.title: Using const
slides.codeSnippet:
const int myNum = 15;
myNum = 10; // This will cause an error!

quizData.question: What happens if you try to re-assign a new value to a const variable?

options.text: It silently ignores the new value.
The computer throws a compilation error.

quizData.explanation: const variables are read-only and cannot be changed after declaration. Attempting to change them will stop the program from compiling.

slides.title: Best Practices 🌟
slides.content:
You should ALWAYS declare a variable as `const` when you have values that are unlikely to change.

Examples:
* Mathematical constants like PI (`const double PI = 3.14159;`)
* Maximum limits (`const int MAX_PLAYERS = 4;`)

quizData.question: You should use const for variables like the player's score during a game.
quizData.explanation: False! A player's score changes constantly during a game, so it shouldn't be read-only.
